FIRST check docs/learned/index.md for existing documentation on this topic. Read relevant docs before exploring raw files. Then explore codebase as necessary.
I need to understand the existing codebase structure for implementing `erk json objective view` and `erk json objective check` machine commands. Please explore:
1. Read `docs/learned/cli/` docs for CLI patterns 2. Read `docs/learned/architecture/` docs relevant to machine commands 3. Read the existing files: - `src/erk/cli/commands/objective/view_cmd.py` (full file) - `src/erk/cli/commands/objective/check_cmd.py` (full file) - `src/erk/cli/commands/objective/__init__.py` 4. Find and read an existing machine command example - look at `src/erk/cli/commands/json/` directory and especially any `pr/view/` pattern mentioned in the plan 5. Read `src/erk/cli/commands/json/__init__.py` 6. Read the `machine_command` decorator definition
Return ALL file contents in full - I need complete code to work with.

session.assistant: Let me read the most relevant CLI documentation and then the source files:
session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→title: "Adding Machine JSON Commands" 3→read_when: 4→ - "adding a new machine-readable JSON command" 5→ - "exposing a command via MCP" 6→ - "creating result dataclasses for JSON output" 7→ - "stacking @mcp_exposed and @machine_command decorators" 8→ - "adding a new CLI command with both human and machine output" 9→ - "creating a new command subpackage" 10→tripwires: 11→ - action: "placing @mcp_exposed below @machine_command in decorator stack" 12→ warning: "@mcp_exposed must be ABOVE @machine_command. Correct order: @mcp_exposed > @machine_command > @click.command." 13→ - action: "creating a result dataclass without to_json_dict() method" 14→ warning: "Result dataclasses should implement to_json_dict() for custom serialization. Without it, the serializer falls back to dataclasses.asdict() which may not handle complex types correctly." 15→ - action: "adding --json flag to a human command" 16→ warning: "Machine JSON output belongs in the `erk json` command tree. Create a machine adapter as json_cli.py in the command's subpackage instead." 17→ - action: "duplicating fetch/query logic in cli.py instead of calling run_*() from operation.py" 18→ warning: "cli.py MUST delegate to the operation. The operation is the single source of truth for business logic. cli.py only marshals Click flags into the Request and renders the Result. Never re-fetch, re-query, or re-compute what the operation already returns." 19→ - action: "eagerly serializing rich objects in the Result dataclass" 20→ warning: "Result dataclasses should carry rich domain objects (not pre-serialized dicts). Serialization belongs in to_json_dict() only. The human CLI needs rich objects for display; the machine adapter needs to_json_dict() for JSON." 21→--- 22→ 23→# Adding Machine JSON Commands 24→ 25→Step-by-step guide to adding structured JSON output for a CLI command using the `erk json` command tree. 26→ 27→## Architecture 28→ 29→Machine commands live in a separate `erk json` tree, not as flags on human commands: 30→
... [149 lines omitted] ...
49→ run_foo(ctx, request) → Result | MachineCommandError 70→from erk_shared.agentclick.machine_command import MachineCommandError 85→def run_foo(ctx: ErkContext, request: FooRequest) -> FooResult | MachineCommandError: 87→ return MachineCommandError(error_type="invalid_input", message="Name required") 99→from erk_shared.agentclick.machine_command import MachineCommandError, machine_command 106→def json_foo(ctx: ErkContext, *, request: FooRequest) -> FooResult | MachineCommandError: 132→ if isinstance(result, MachineCommandError): 133→ user_output(click.style(f"Error: {result.message}", fg="red"))

1→--- 2→title: Objective Commands 3→read_when: 4→ - "working with erk objective commands" 5→ - "implementing objective check or close functionality" 6→ - "understanding objective validation patterns" 7→last_audited: "2026-02-16 14:20 PT" 8→audit_result: clean 9→tripwires: 10→ - action: "displaying user-provided text in Rich CLI tables without escaping" 11→ warning: "Use `escape_markup(value)` for user data in Rich tables. Brackets like `[text]` are interpreted as style tags and will disappear." 12→--- 13→ 14→# Objective Commands 15→ 16→## Permission Mode Override Pattern 17→ 18→<!-- Source: packages/erk-shared/src/erk_shared/context/types.py, InteractiveAgentConfig.with_overrides --> 19→<!-- Source: src/erk/cli/commands/objective/plan_cmd.py, plan --> 20→ 21→The `with_overrides()` method on `InteractiveAgentConfig` allows selective override of config values: 22→ 23→- Pass a value (e.g., `"plan"`) to force that mode 24→- Pass `None` to preserve the config file value 25→ 26→The `plan` command forces `permission_mode_override="plan"` to ensure the agent explores and plans rather than immediately executing. The `--dangerous` flag conditionally overrides `allow_dangerous_override`, allowing users to opt into skipping permission prompts. 27→ 28→## Validation Check Design 29→ 30→<!-- Source: src/erk/cli/commands/objective/check_cmd.py, validate_objective -->
... [103 lines omitted] ...
32→The `validate_objective()` function returns a discriminated union — either `ObjectiveValidationSuccess` or `ObjectiveValidationError`. This allows callers to distinguish between "couldn't validate" (network error, issue not found) and "validation completed but found problems" (orphaned statuses, inconsistent numbering). 36→**Why both a passed field AND a checks list**: The `passed` field is the overall result (false if any check failed). The `checks` list provides granular detail about which specific rules failed and why. JSON consumers can filter or reprocess the list; human output can show a summary. 54→Phases are validated for sequential ordering by `(number, suffix)` tuples. This allows sub-phases like `1A, 1B, 1C` followed by `2` without triggering an error, but catches cases like `[1, 3, 2]` or `[1A, 1C, 1B]`. 127→- [Discriminated Union Error Handling](../architecture/discriminated-union-error-handling.md) - Result type patterns used by `validate_objective()`

1→"""Validate an objective's format and roadmap consistency.""" 2→ 3→import json 4→from dataclasses import dataclass 5→ 6→import click 7→ 8→from erk.cli.alias import alias 9→from erk.cli.github_parsing import parse_issue_identifier 10→from erk.cli.repo_resolution import get_remote_github, resolved_repo_option 11→from erk.core.context import ErkContext 12→from erk_shared.gateway.github.issues.types import IssueNotFound 13→from erk_shared.gateway.github.metadata.core import ( 14→ find_metadata_block, 15→ has_metadata_block, 16→) 17→from erk_shared.gateway.github.metadata.dependency_graph import ( 18→ DependencyGraph, 19→ build_graph, 20→ compute_graph_summary, 21→ find_graph_next_node, 22→ phases_from_graph, 23→) 24→from erk_shared.gateway.github.metadata.plan_header import ( 25→ extract_plan_header_objective_issue, 26→) 27→from erk_shared.gateway.github.metadata.roadmap import ( 28→ parse_roadmap, 29→ rerender_comment_roadmap, 30→ serialize_phases,
... [407 lines omitted] ...
42→ """Validation completed (may have passed or failed checks). 47→ failed_count: Number of failed checks 48→ graph: Dependency graph of roadmap nodes (empty if parsing failed) 51→ validation_errors: Parser-level warnings from roadmap parsing 57→ failed_count: int 61→ validation_errors: list[str] 66→class ObjectiveValidationError: 69→ error: str 72→ObjectiveValidationResult = ObjectiveValidationSuccess | ObjectiveValidationError 177→ ObjectiveValidationError if unable to complete validation 184→ return ObjectiveValidationError(error=f"Issue #{issue_number} not found") 194→ phases, validation_errors = parse_roadmap(issue.body) 201→ failed_count = sum(1 for passed, _ in checks if not passed) 203→ passed=failed_count == 0, 205→ failed_count=failed_count, 209→ validation_errors=[], 216→ # Block exists but failed to parse 217→ checks.append((False, f"Roadmap parses successfully ({'; '.join(validation_errors)})")) 218→ failed_count = sum(1 for passed, _ in checks if not passed) 222→ failed_count=failed_count, 226→ validation_errors=validation_errors, 307→ failed_count = sum(1 for passed, _ in checks if not passed) 310→ passed=failed_count == 0, 312→ failed_count=failed_count, 316→ validation_errors=validation_errors, 364→ if isinstance(result, ObjectiveValidationError): 369→ "error": result.error, 388→ "validation_errors": result.validation_errors, 399→ if isinstance(result, ObjectiveValidationError): 401→ click.style("Error: ", fg="red") + f"Failed to validate objective: {result.error}" 425→ check_word = "checks" if result.failed_count > 1 else "check" 428→ f"Objective validation failed ({result.failed_count} {check_word} failed)", fg="red"

1→"""Objective management commands.""" 2→ 3→import click 4→ 5→from erk.cli.alias import register_with_aliases 6→from erk.cli.commands.objective.check_cmd import check_objective 7→from erk.cli.commands.objective.close_cmd import close_objective 8→from erk.cli.commands.objective.list_cmd import list_objectives 9→from erk.cli.commands.objective.plan_cmd import plan_objective 10→from erk.cli.commands.objective.view_cmd import view_objective 11→from erk.cli.help_formatter import ErkCommandGroup 12→ 13→ 14→@click.group("objective", cls=ErkCommandGroup, grouped=False) 15→def objective_group() -> None: 16→ """Manage objectives (multi-PR coordination issues).""" 17→ pass 18→ 19→ 20→register_with_aliases(objective_group, check_objective) 21→register_with_aliases(objective_group, close_objective) 22→register_with_aliases(objective_group, plan_objective) 23→register_with_aliases(objective_group, list_objectives) 24→register_with_aliases(objective_group, view_objective) 25→

1→"""Machine-readable JSON command tree (erk json ...). 2→ 3→All commands in this tree accept JSON input from stdin and produce 4→structured JSON output. 1→"""Core operation for one-shot dispatch (transport-independent). 2→ 3→Contains the request type and operation function that both the 4→human command (one_shot/cli.py) and machine command (one_shot/json_cli.py) share. 5→""" 6→ 7→from dataclasses import dataclass 8→ 9→from erk.cli.commands.implement_shared import normalize_model_name 10→from erk.cli.commands.one_shot_remote_dispatch import ( 11→ OneShotDispatchParams, 12→ OneShotDispatchResult, 13→ OneShotDryRunResult, 14→ dispatch_one_shot_remote, 15→) 16→from erk.cli.commands.ref_resolution import resolve_dispatch_ref 17→from erk.cli.ensure import UserFacingCliError 18→from erk.core.context import ErkContext, NoRepoSentinel 19→from erk_shared.agentclick.machine_command import MachineCommandError 20→from erk_shared.gateway.remote_github.abc import RemoteGitHub 21→from erk_shared.gateway.remote_github.real import RealRemoteGitHub 22→ 23→ 24→@dataclass(frozen=True) 25→class OneShotRequest: 26→ """Request type for one-shot dispatch.""" 27→ 28→ prompt: str 29→ model: str | None = None 30→ dry_run: bool = False

1→"""Machine command decorator for pure-JSON CLI commands. 2→ 3→Provides @machine_command for commands that: 4→- Accept JSON input from stdin (not Click params) 5→- Return structured JSON output 6→- Use frozen dataclass request types for strict validation 7→- Support --schema flag for introspection 8→ 9→Unlike @json_command (which adds --json/--schema to human commands), 10→@machine_command creates dedicated machine-only commands with no 11→Click-based input parsing. The request type IS the input schema. 12→""" 13→ 14→import json 15→from dataclasses import dataclass 16→from typing import Any 17→ 18→import click 19→ 20→from erk_shared.agentclick.dataclass_json import ( 21→ emit_json_error, 22→ emit_json_success, 23→ parse_dataclass_from_json, 24→ read_json_stdin, 25→ serialize_to_json_dict, 26→) 27→ 28→ 29→@dataclass(frozen=True) 30→class MachineCommandMeta:
... [125 lines omitted] ...
38→class MachineCommandError: 39→ """Structured error result from a machine command.""" 41→ error_type: str 59→ to_json_dict()) or a MachineCommandError. The decorator handles 79→ json.JSONDecodeError: If stdin contains invalid JSON 80→ ValueError: If stdin JSON is not an object 99→ ValueError: On validation errors (unknown fields, missing required, type mismatch) 104→def emit_machine_error(error: MachineCommandError) -> None: 105→ """Emit a structured error to stdout.""" 106→ emit_json_error(error_type=error.error_type, message=error.message)

session.tool_result: total 92 drwxr-xr-x 3 runner runner 4096 Mar 10 13:30 . drwxr-xr-x 22 runner runner 4096 Mar 10 13:30 .. -rw-r--r-- 1 runner runner 915 Mar 10 13:30 __init__.py drwxr-xr-x 2 runner runner 4096 Mar 10 13:30 __pycache__ -rw-r--r-- 1 runner runner 14997 Mar 10 13:30 check_cmd.py -rw-r--r-- 1 runner runner 2331 Mar 10 13:30 close_cmd.py -rw-r--r-- 1 runner runner 5958 Mar 10 13:30 list_cmd.py -rw-r--r-- 1 runner runner 29211 Mar 10 13:30 plan_cmd.py -rw-r--r-- 1 runner runner 15431 Mar 10 13:30 view_cmd.py
session.assistant: Let me check the current plan in the branch to understand what's needed:
session.tool_result:
# Plan: Create `erk json objective view` and `erk json objective check` machine commands
Part of Objective #9009, Node 3.2
## Context
The "best-of-both-worlds" machine command architecture (PR #9132) separates human CLI commands from machine-readable JSON commands. Human commands live at `erk <path>` with rich output; machine commands live at `erk json <path>` with structured JSON I/O via `@machine_command`.
Currently, `erk objective view` and `erk objective check` have `--json-output` flags inline — the legacy pattern. This plan migrates them to the new architecture: extract shared operations into `operation.py`, create thin `json_cli.py` machine adapters, and register them under `erk json objective view/check`.
## Implementation
### Phase 1: Extract `objective view` operation
**Create `src/erk/cli/commands/objective/view/operation.py`**
Extract from `view_cmd.py`: - `ObjectiveViewRequest` frozen dataclass: `identifier: str | None = None` (optional, infers from branch) - `ObjectiveViewResult` frozen dataclass with fields: `issue_number`, `phases` (serialized), `graph_nodes`, `unblocked`, `pending_unblocked`, `next_node`, `is_complete`, `summary`. Include `to_json_dict()` matching the current `_display_json` output shape. - `run_objective_view()` function: the shared logic from `view_objective()` that fetches the issue, validates the label, parses the roadmap, builds the graph, and returns `ObjectiveViewResult | MachineCommandError`.
**Refactor `view_cmd.py` → `src/erk/cli/commands/objective/view/cli.py`**
Move the human command to the new location. It imports from `operation.py` and calls `run_objective_view()`, then renders with Rich tables (all existing `_format_*` and display functions move here).
**Create `src/erk/cli/commands/objective/view/json_cli.py`**
Thin machine adapter following the pr/view pattern: ```python @machine_command(request_type=ObjectiveViewRequest, output_types=(ObjectiveViewResult,))
... [70 lines omitted] ...
Keep `validate_objective()`, `ObjectiveValidationSuccess`, `ObjectiveValidationError`, and the `_check_*` helper functions in a shared module (either keep them in `check_cmd.py` and import, or move to `check/validation.py`). Prefer moving to `check/validation.py` since it's pure logic. Keep the `--json-output` flags on the human commands for backwards compatibility during transition, but have them emit a deprecation warning pointing to `erk json objective view/check`. The flags delegate to the same `run_*` operation.
session.assistant: Perfect! Now let me read the PR view cli.py to see how the human command interacts with the operation:
session.tool_result:
1→"""Command to fetch and display a single plan. 2→ 3→Human-facing command with Click options and rich terminal output. 4→Machine-readable JSON equivalent lives in pr/view/json_cli.py. 5→""" 6→ 7→from datetime import datetime 8→ 9→import click 10→ 11→from erk.cli.commands.pr.view.operation import PrViewRequest, PrViewResult, run_pr_view 12→from erk.cli.repo_resolution import resolved_repo_option 13→from erk.core.context import ErkContext 14→from erk_shared.agentclick.machine_command import MachineCommandError 15→from erk_shared.core.typing_utils import narrow_to_literal 16→from erk_shared.gateway.github.metadata.schemas import ( 17→ CREATED_BY, 18→ CREATED_FROM_SESSION, 19→ LAST_DISPATCHED_AT, 20→ LAST_DISPATCHED_RUN_ID, 21→ LAST_LEARN_SESSION, 22→ LAST_LOCAL_IMPL_AT, 23→ LAST_LOCAL_IMPL_EVENT, 24→ LAST_LOCAL_IMPL_SESSION, 25→ LAST_LOCAL_IMPL_USER, 26→ LAST_REMOTE_IMPL_AT, 27→ LEARN_PLAN_ISSUE, 28→ LEARN_PLAN_PR, 29→ LEARN_RUN_ID, 30→ LEARN_STATUS,
... [55 lines omitted] ...
session.assistant: Perfect! Now let me create a comprehensive summary document of all the files. Let me read a bit more of the PR view cli to see the full command structure:
session.tool_result:
80→def _format_header_section(header_info: dict[str, object], *, plan_url: str | None) -> list[str]: 81→ """Format the header info section for display.""" 82→ lines: list[str] = [] 83→ 84→ if not header_info: 85→ return lines 86→ 87→ lines.append("") 88→ lines.append(click.style("\u2500\u2500\u2500 Header \u2500\u2500\u2500", bold=True)) 89→ 90→ if CREATED_BY in header_info: 91→ lines.append(_format_field("Created by", str(header_info[CREATED_BY]))) 92→ 93→ if SCHEMA_VERSION in header_info: 94→ lines.append(_format_field("Schema version", str(header_info[SCHEMA_VERSION]))) 95→ 96→ if WORKTREE_NAME in header_info: 97→ lines.append(_format_field("Worktree", str(header_info[WORKTREE_NAME]))) 98→ 99→ if OBJECTIVE_ISSUE in header_info: 100→ lines.append(_format_field("Objective", f"#{header_info[OBJECTIVE_ISSUE]}")) 101→ 102→ if SOURCE_REPO in header_info: 103→ lines.append(_format_field("Source repo", str(header_info[SOURCE_REPO]))) 104→ 105→ has_local_impl = any( 106→ k in header_info 107→ for k in [LAST_LOCAL_IMPL_AT, LAST_LOCAL_IMPL_EVENT, LAST_LOCAL_IMPL_SESSION] 108→ ) 109→ if has_local_impl:
... [25 lines omitted] ...
